text: We present a video of robotic anal sphincter repair. A seventy-one-year-old female presented with urge and pacifical incontinence after obstructive anal sphincter injury who had a shortened anal sphincter and virtually absent perineal body and had a high-grade internal prolapse as well. She was initially managed conservatively but unfortunately she didn't respond and after initial investigations she was discussed in MDT where the decision was made to operate upon her. An erectile physiology study showed Her mean inner sphincter pressure was of mercury and a maximum pressure of and there was an asymmetry of Rectal pressure versus volume graph showed passive leak throughout. Proctogram was done at the start and late which showed interception, interocele and cystocele as well. Pre-operative surgery we did an endo-anal ultrasound which showed that there was a defect from nine to three o'clock position.

The video shows a pre-operative setup with the patient in a lithotomy position. The surgeon is at the robotic console, and the assistant is at the bedside. Four eight-millimeter robotic ports are positioned at the umbilical level, along with a twelve-millimeter assistant port. The rectum and vagina prolapse are visible. The dissection begins with the retraction of the sigmoid colon and marking of the peritoneum.

The surgeon begins dissection from medial to lateral, ensuring not to enter the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The peritoneum is visibly reflected, and dissection proceeds anteriorly. The vagina, previously subjected to hysterectomy, is retracted using Kelly's retractor as the dissection continues lower down.

The surgeon carefully dissects to separate the rectum from the vagina, approaching the pelvic floor. The rectum is completely separated, revealing the pelvic floor and clearly visible sphincters with the aid of anal dilators.
The surgeon continues the dissection lower down, identifying the damaged anal sphincter. The sphincter is sutured using PDS sutures, with careful attention to the surrounding tissue.

The repaired sphincters are clearly visible. The surgeon proceeds with mesh rectopexy using a porcelain mesh, which is sutured to the rectum starting from a point two centimeters proximal to the distal end. Approximately five to six sutures are placed, and the distal end of the mesh is secured to prevent migration.

The surgeon fixes the mesh around the rectum, securing it anteriorly and laterally. The mesh is then attached to the sacral promontory with two to three sutures, taking care to avoid vessel damage. A stitch is placed at the distal part, and the vagina is stitched to the mesh and rectum to prevent vaginal prolapse. Finally, the peritoneal defect is closed to prevent any small bowel from entering the pelvic cavity.

At the end of the procedure, repeat endo-anal ultrasound was performed which showed marked improvement from before. The patient will be followed up at six weeks and six months with anorectal physiology and endo-anal ultrasound and also quality of life questionnaire will be given. In the end, robotic anal sphincter repair is feasible and suitable for patients with pelvic floor disruption requiring abdominal approach. Modification of the technique could be applied to levatorplasty and other forms of pelvic floor repair. Combining the procedure with rectopexy could reduce the risk of post-operative incontinence.
